topic: An artist's work is displayed without their permission
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, sharp and unforgiving, illuminating my canvas—my very soul laid bare. I stood near the entrance, my hands clenched so tightly my knuckles were white, watching strangers move past the vibrant chaos I had poured onto the linen. Each appreciative glance felt like an accusation. *This isn't right. They don't understand the process. They only see the finished, flawed product.* A low thrum of anxiety vibrated in my chest, a constant reminder of the exposure. I wanted to melt into the shadows, to erase the evidence of my vulnerability before anyone could properly dissect it. When a woman stopped, her expression was polite, distant. I offered a small, jerky nod, the smile I intended felt brittle and entirely manufactured. I retreated quickly, the sound of my own hurried footsteps echoing loudly in the cavernous space, desperate to escape the scrutiny.

[story 2]

The velvet rope felt like a barrier I couldn't cross, a tangible representation of the boundary I desperately wished to maintain. I lingered near the back, pretending to examine a nearby sculpture, my gaze darting constantly toward the brightly lit wall where my work hung. My breath hitched in shallow gasps; the air felt thick and suffocating. Every time someone paused, their silence seemed to amplify the internal monologue of inadequacy. *It’s all just clumsy strokes and wasted time. Nobody values this mess.* I shifted my weight from foot to foot, a nervous tic I couldn't suppress, trying to project an aura of nonchalance that felt utterly false. When a critic approached, their tone was measured, dissecting every brushstroke with clinical detachment. I managed a strained, overly cheerful response, the effort making my throat feel tight and dry. I wanted to scream that this was mine, that it was a private language, but the words caught, leaving only a hollow ache behind my ribs.

[story 3]

I watched from across the room, a ghost observing my own creation. The gallery was too bright, the chatter too loud, and the silence emanating from my own existence felt deafening. My palms were slick against my trousers as I stood near a pillar, meticulously cataloging every minute detail of the surrounding environment, as if finding an escape route in the architecture. *They think they own this space now. They think they own the interpretation.* A wave of profound disappointment washed over me, heavy and cold. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of shame that wanted to burn through my skin. When a group gathered around one piece, their murmurs were analytical, dissecting technique and intent. I felt a deep, visceral urge to flee, to dissolve into the anonymity of the city streets. I clenched my jaw until it ached, forcing my posture rigid, trying to appear unbothered, a performance of composure that felt like a costume I was failing to wear correctly.
